title: Two Elams
bullets: The single most important thing to know about Elam is that it was two places, not one — a lowland half at Susa on the Khuzestan plain and a highland half at Anshan up in the mountain basins of Fars, economically and politically bound together across the Zagros for almost three thousand years.

The two-Elam geography was the kingdom's most reliable strategic asset — every time a Mesopotamian army destroyed Susa, the Elamite kings retreated into the highlands and waited, and eventually came back down to rebuild.

Susa is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on Earth, with earliest village layers going back to 4200 BCE — older than any Sumerian city except Eridu, older than Egyptian Memphis, older than anything in the Indus Valley.

The Proto-Elamite script appeared on clay tablets at Susa around 3100 BCE, essentially contemporary with the earliest Sumerian proto-cuneiform at Uruk, early Egyptian hieroglyphs, and the first Indus proto-signs — four civilizations all inventing writing in the same two-century window.

Anshan in the Zagros reached roughly 500 acres of walled urban area by 2700 BCE, putting it in the same size class as contemporary Uruk — a major Bronze Age city a thousand miles from any other comparable urban center.

Elamite is a language isolate with no known relatives — one of three isolates in the Bronze Age Near East alongside Sumerian and Kassite, a cluster of linguistic loners modern linguists still can't explain.

The cult of Inshushinak at Susa ran for more than two thousand years — documented from around 2500 BCE, still alive at the Assyrian destruction of Susa in 646 BCE, and traceable into the Achaemenid period as late as 500 BCE.

Queen Puabi's headdress and the other Royal Tombs of Ur were full of Badakhshani lapis lazuli, Harappan carnelian, and Anatolian/Iranian gold — the wealth of Sumer's most famous royal cemetery came overland through Elamite caravans and maritime through the Indus network.

title: Naram-Sin's Stone
bullets: Sargon of Akkad's armies reached Susa around 2300 BCE, captured Awan, and briefly made Elam a tribute province of the world's first empire.

The Elamite-Akkadian Treaty, preserved on a clay tablet in the Louvre and dated to roughly 2250 BCE, is the oldest surviving political treaty in any language from any civilization — the famous Egypt-Hittite treaty of Ramesses II comes nearly a thousand years later.

The treaty invokes more than thirty gods as witnesses — both the Akkadian pantheon (Enlil, Shamash, Ishtar) and the Elamite pantheon (Inshushinak, Humban, Pinikir) — and is our earliest real list of Elamite deities.
Naram-Sin, Sargon's grandson, was the first king in Mesopotamian history to write his name with the divine determinative — claiming to be a living god rather than a mere mortal ruler.

Naram-Sin broke the treaty and sacked Susa a second time; his Victory Stele — carved to celebrate a separate campaign against the Lullubi mountain people in the same campaign cycle — is the same stele Shutruk-Nahhunte would drag back to Susa as war loot more than a thousand years later.

Around 2200 BCE, someone at Susa invented Linear Elamite — Elam's second independent writing system, partially deciphered in 2020 by François Desset after more than a century as an unsolved linguistic mystery.

Puzur-Inshushinak, last great king of the Awan dynasty, briefly campaigned west into a fragmenting Akkadian Empire around 2100 BCE and proclaimed himself 'king of the four quarters of the world' — the Elamite script got its richest surviving corpus from his bilingual royal statues.

title: Ur Falls
bullets: In 2004 BCE the Elamite king Kindattu of the Shimashki dynasty led his army into Ur, sacked and burned the capital of the Third Dynasty of Ur, and carried the last Sumerian king Ibbi-Sin back to Anshan in chains.

The sack of Ur is the accidental end of Sumerian political history — the Amorites flooded into the vacated plain, Sumer became a dead scholarly language, and the whole spoken Sumerian world ended with the fall of Ur.
Sumerian priests composed the Lament for Ur, the oldest surviving city-lament in world literature — a grief document blaming the Elamites and the goddess Ningal weeping over the ruins of her temple.

The Shimashki kings introduced the distinctive Elamite succession pattern — fraternal and avuncular transfers through ranked offices rather than father-to-son inheritance — a system whose closest ancient parallel is Shang dynasty China on the opposite side of Asia.

After the sack, the Elamite court adopted Mesopotamian cuneiform for writing the Elamite language — awkwardly, because cuneiform was designed for Sumerian and Akkadian sound systems — and Linear Elamite fell out of use entirely by 1950 BCE.

The Elamite pantheon crystallized into a divine family: Humban as king of the gods, Kiririsha as his consort and great mother, Nahhunte as the sun god and divine judge, Inshushinak as patron of Susa, and Pinikir as the Venus sky goddess.

Humban's name became a theophoric element in Elamite royal names for more than 1,500 years — the last king to bear a Humban-name appears in Achaemenid texts from the fifth century BCE, long after Elam itself was politically absorbed.

The Elamites looted the cult statue of Nanaya from Uruk during these wars and installed her in Susa, where she stayed for more than 1,600 years — until Ashurbanipal fought a war in the seventh century BCE specifically to bring her home.

title: Grand Regents
bullets: Around 1850 BCE, a man named Eparti I founded a dynasty whose rulers took the title sukkalmah — 'grand regent' or literally 'great vizier' — presenting themselves as servants of the divine family rather than as god-kings in the Mesopotamian style.

The Sukkalmah dynasty ran Elam through a three-office collective structure: sukkalmah at the top, sukkal of Elam and Simashki ruling the highlands, sukkal of Susa ruling the lowlands, with brothers and uncles rotating horizontally through all three offices before the next generation took over.

This is the opposite of how Mesopotamian kingship worked — and unlike any other ancient Near Eastern political system. It gave Elam three hundred years of unusual political stability while Mesopotamia went through a dozen dynastic crises.

The Mari letters, a hoard of 25,000 clay tablets excavated from Zimri-Lim's palace in Syria, show the Elamite sukkalmah Siwe-palar-hupak addressed as 'father' by Zimri-Lim of Mari and deferred to by Hammurabi's own officials — Elam was at that moment the diplomatic center of the ancient Near East.

Hammurabi of Babylon was an Elamite client king for the first fifteen years of his reign, addressing Siwe-palar-hupak as his diplomatic 'father,' before switching sides around 1764 BCE and launching the wars that built the Old Babylonian Empire.

Hammurabi defeated Siwe-palar-hupak in battle and pushed Elam back behind Susa, but did not destroy the kingdom or sack the capital — the Sukkalmah dynasty continued to rule Elam for another two hundred years.

Susa under the Sukkalmah produced the earliest substantial corpus of ordinary people's documents from any Elamite site — thousands of cuneiform legal contracts recording sales, marriages, inheritances, and lawsuits in a middle-class urban society.

Elamite women had more direct legal agency than their Babylonian counterparts — the contracts show wives owning property in their own names, initiating lawsuits, and inheriting from parents on equal terms with brothers.

title: The Ziggurat Generation
bullets: Around 1400 BCE a new royal line called the Igihalkid dynasty emerged from the highlands and rebuilt Elam into one of the three or four great powers of the Late Bronze Age Near East, alongside New Kingdom Egypt, the Hittite Empire, Kassite Babylonia, and the Middle Assyrian state.

Untash-Napirisha, greatest of the Igihalkid kings, ruled around 1275-1240 BCE and launched the most ambitious building program in Elamite history — founding a new sacred city called Dur-Untash in the desert 25 miles southeast of Susa.

The Ziggurat of Chogha Zanbil at Dur-Untash is the largest ziggurat standing anywhere in the world today — roughly 174 feet tall, 345 feet square at the base, built of an estimated 300 million bricks each stamped with Untash-Napirisha's name.

It is also the last great ziggurat of the ancient world — nobody after the twelfth century BCE ever built one this big again, and Chogha Zanbil is the final flowering of a 1,500-year-old Mesopotamian architectural tradition.

While Untash-Napirisha was finishing Chogha Zanbil, Ramesses II was carving the colossal rock-cut temples of Abu Simbel into the cliffs of Nubia — two Late Bronze Age monument-building competitions running in parallel at opposite ends of the Near East.

Untash-Napirisha's wife Queen Napir-Asu is commemorated in a headless life-sized bronze statue weighing 3,760 pounds — nearly two tons cast solid in a single piece, specifically solid so no thief could melt it down for the metal.

The Napir-Asu bronze has a contemporary counterpart in the Houmuwu Ding from Shang dynasty China, a 1,832-pound ritual cauldron cast in piece-molds at Anyang around 1200 BCE — two unrelated civilizations solving the same monumental-bronze problem at opposite ends of Asia in the same century.

title: Sack of Babylon
bullets: In 1158 BCE the Elamite king Shutruk-Nahhunte led an army across the Zagros, defeated the last Kassite king Zababa-shuma-iddin, breached the walls of Babylon, and conducted one of the richest looting operations in the entire ancient world.

Among the trophies Shutruk-Nahhunte dragged back to Susa were two of the most famous Mesopotamian artifacts in existence: the Code of Hammurabi (carved around 1754 BCE) and the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin (carved around 2250 BCE) — and both would stay at Susa for the next three thousand years.
The Code of Hammurabi would not exist today if Shutruk-Nahhunte had not carried it to Susa — left in Sippar it would have been destroyed by later wars or recycled for its expensive black diorite.

Shutruk-Nahhunte also seized the cult statue of Marduk himself, the Babylonian chief god — the third documented kidnapping of the same statue, after the Hittite Mursili I in 1595 BCE and the Assyrian Tukulti-Ninurta I around 1225 BCE.

Shutruk-Nahhunte's son Kutir-Nahhunte III continued his father's wars and briefly installed Elamite governors over several Babylonian cities for about a decade — the closest Elam ever came to actually ruling Mesopotamia as subject territory.

Shilhak-Inshushinak, Kutir-Nahhunte's brother and successor, commissioned the Sit Shamshi bronze tableau — a 23-inch miniature sculpture showing two naked priests performing a sunrise ritual at a bronze temple complex, the clearest surviving window into Elamite religious practice.

The Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar I — not the famous Neo-Babylonian king of the same name six centuries later — organized the counterattack, defeated Hutelutush-Inshushinak, sacked Susa, and recovered the Marduk statue for Babylon.

Nebuchadnezzar I did not recover the Code of Hammurabi or the Victory Stele of Naram-Sin during the sack — either he could not find them in the rubble or he was focused on Marduk — and both would stay at Susa until the French excavated them at the turn of the twentieth century (the Naram-Sin stele in 1898, the Code of Hammurabi in 1901).

title: Til-Tuba
bullets: The Neo-Elamite period replaced the old Sukkalmah-style centralized state with a loose confederation of highland and lowland city-states — each with its own royal line, all sharing the old pantheon and scribal tradition — the first three and a half centuries of which are a near-silent 'Elamite Dark Age' (roughly 1100–750 BCE) before the kingdom reappears in the eighth century BCE.

Between 745 and 612 BCE the Neo-Assyrian Empire conquered almost the entire ancient Near East with the first military-industrial complex in world history: professional standing army, iron weaponry, mass cavalry, siege engineering, and forced population transfer as a strategic tool.

Ashurbanipal ruled Assyria from 668 to 631 BCE and is one of the most complicated figures in ancient history — a brutal warrior who flayed enemies alive and boasted about it in palace reliefs, and also the scholar who built the 30,000-tablet Royal Library at Nineveh that preserved most of Sumerian and Akkadian civilization.

In 653 BCE the Elamite king Te-Umman raised an army, crossed the Tigris, and attacked Assyrian territory — Ashurbanipal met him at Til-Tuba and destroyed the Elamite force in a battle whose grisly details survive on a series of narrative relief panels in the British Museum.

The Til-Tuba reliefs show Te-Umman's death frame by frame like a comic book: an arrow through his shoulder, his chariot losing control, his fall, his son's failed defense, and finally an Assyrian soldier stepping forward with a sword to sever his head on the battlefield.

The most famous panel is Ashurbanipal's Garden Party Relief, showing the king and queen reclining on couches drinking wine in the royal garden at Nineveh while the severed head of Te-Umman dangles from a tree branch directly above them.

The Elamite king Humban-Nikash II then allied with Ashurbanipal's own rebelling brother Shamash-shum-ukin of Babylon — probably the worst political decision any Elamite king ever made, because it invited the full Assyrian response.

In 646 BCE Ashurbanipal sacked Susa, stripped every temple, opened the royal tombs and carried the bones of Elamite kings back to Assyria so their ghosts 'should find no rest,' and reportedly salted the agricultural fields — the same trope later attached to Rome's destruction of Carthage.

title: The Persian Echo
bullets: Here is the fact most general histories miss: the founder of the Persian Empire was, in his own self-presentation, a king of Anshan — Cyrus the Great's Cyrus Cylinder chains four generations of his royal line (himself, his father, grandfather, and great-grandfather) together as kings of Anshan, 'of an enduring royal line.'

When the Persians migrated into Fars in the seventh century BCE, they found Anshan already there as a long-established Elamite highland capital, and rather than destroying it they absorbed the bureaucracy, married into the royal families, and adopted the Elamite royal title 'king of Anshan' as their own.

Cyrus the Great conquered the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BCE and built the largest political unit the ancient world had yet seen — an empire of roughly 50 million people stretching from Egypt to the Indus River.

The bureaucratic working language of Cyrus's Persian Empire was not Old Persian or Aramaic but Elamite — written in the same cuneiform scribal tradition that had been the Elamite royal standard since the Shimashki dynasty of 2000 BCE.

The Persepolis Fortification Archive is a hoard of roughly 30,000 clay tablets preserved by the fire when Alexander the Great burned Persepolis in 330 BCE — all of them written in Elamite, recording daily rations, wages, and royal logistics under Darius I.

The Persians, the Greeks, the Romans, and the Indo-Aryans of the Indus Valley are all descended from the same Indo-European population movement — Persian and Sanskrit are sister languages from the Indo-Iranian branch that split around the Hindu Kush.

Darius I's Behistun Inscription, carved on a cliff 200 feet above the royal road around 520 BCE, exists in three languages side by side: Old Persian, Babylonian, and Elamite — and the Elamite version was carved first, reflecting its status as the working language of the imperial bureaucracy.

Behistun became the Rosetta Stone of cuneiform: Henry Rawlinson climbed the cliff on ropes in 1837 to make paper impressions, and the Old Persian version he copied eventually cracked Babylonian, which then cracked Sumerian and every other cuneiform script in the ancient Near East.

The ghosts of the Elamite kings did not find rest, but their language and their scribes and their administrative system lived on as the skeleton of the largest empire the ancient world had ever built — Elam never actually died, it became the bureaucratic spine of Persia.
